text.title: Spec Writing Framework
text.summary: Seven-step process for writing a product specification that separates what from how, includes testable acceptance criteria, and feeds directly into SDD execution.

rule.statement: A spec answers WHAT to build and WHY. Design documents answer HOW. Never mix them: requirements describe observable behavior, not implementation choices.
rule.rationale: Specs that include implementation details become obsolete with every architecture decision and force re-review. "The system shall use Postgres" is a design decision; "The system shall return results within 200ms at p95" is a requirement.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Answer four context questions before writing: why are we building this, who requested it, what happens if we don't, and how does this fit product strategy?
rule.rationale: Context anchors the spec to a business reason and prevents scope drift during stakeholder review.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Write the problem statement: who has it, what specifically it is, how they solve it today, and what the impact is.
rule.rationale: A vague problem statement produces vague requirements. Specificity in the problem propagates to testability in the acceptance criteria.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Write 3–5 goals with measurable current and target values. Write Non-Goals with ≥ 3 explicit exclusions. Non-Goals is mandatory and non-empty.
rule.rationale: Goals without numbers cannot be used to declare success. Non-Goals without items invite scope creep on every review cycle.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Write user stories in "As a [user], I want to [action], so that [benefit]" format. Include the "so that" — it is the motivation, not optional.
rule.rationale: User stories without "so that" become feature requests with no user context, making prioritization arbitrary.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Assign every functional requirement a unique ID (FR-N) and a M/S/C priority. Every NFR must include a numeric criterion (latency, error rate, uptime).
rule.rationale: FRs without IDs cannot be tracked. NFRs without numbers ("the system shall be fast") are unverifiable and will be interpreted differently by every reader.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Write Given-When-Then acceptance criteria for every functional requirement. These become test cases — they must be executable, not descriptive.
rule.rationale: The faion-feature-executor skill consumes acceptance criteria as its test specification. Vague criteria ("works correctly") produce vague tests that pass trivially.

rule.statement: Step 7 — List Open Questions as honest unknowns. Never write "none" unless you have explicitly verified all knowns. Minimum: 1 question or a note "verified all knowns."
rule.rationale: Agents hallucinate answers to unasked questions. Forcing Open Questions to the surface creates a checkpoint for human review before the spec reaches "Approved."

rule.statement: Cap most specs at 1–3 pages. If longer, split into sub-features. Version the spec: update status field (Draft / Review / Approved / Superseded) on every significant change.
rule.rationale: Specs longer than 3 pages are skimmed, not read. Status versioning ensures stakeholders know which version they reviewed and approved.

text.reference: Full PRD template with all sections
Spec linter: validates FR IDs, priorities, AC coverage
